---
title: "Competition, Scoring, and Health Outcomes in School Food Procurement"
subtitle: "Evidence from FSMC Procurement Auctions"
author: "Andrew Swenson"
institute: "Stanford Health Policy"
date: today
format:
  beamer:
    theme: Madrid
    colortheme: default
    fonttheme: default
    aspectratio: 169
    navigation: horizontal
    section-titles: true
    keep-tex: false
    include-in-header:
      text: |
        % ── Stanford color palette ──────────────────────────────────────────────
        \definecolor{StanfordCardinal}{RGB}{140,21,21}
        \definecolor{StanfordDark}{RGB}{98,15,15}
        \definecolor{StanfordCool}{RGB}{0,152,219}
        \definecolor{StanfordWarm}{RGB}{178,111,22}
        \definecolor{StanfordStone}{RGB}{116,116,116}
        \definecolor{StanfordLight}{RGB}{245,235,220}
        \definecolor{StanfordWhite}{RGB}{255,255,255}

        % ── Apply Stanford palette to Beamer theme ───────────────────────────────
        \setbeamercolor{palette primary}{bg=StanfordCardinal, fg=white}
        \setbeamercolor{palette secondary}{bg=StanfordDark, fg=white}
        \setbeamercolor{palette tertiary}{bg=StanfordCardinal, fg=white}
        \setbeamercolor{palette quaternary}{bg=StanfordDark, fg=white}
        \setbeamercolor{structure}{fg=StanfordCardinal}
        \setbeamercolor{section in toc}{fg=StanfordCardinal}
        \setbeamercolor{subsection in toc}{fg=StanfordDark}
        \setbeamercolor{frametitle}{bg=StanfordCardinal, fg=white}
        \setbeamercolor{title}{bg=StanfordCardinal, fg=white}
        \setbeamercolor{subtitle}{fg=StanfordLight}
        \setbeamercolor{author in head/foot}{bg=StanfordDark, fg=white}
        \setbeamercolor{title in head/foot}{bg=StanfordCardinal, fg=white}
        \setbeamercolor{date in head/foot}{bg=StanfordDark, fg=white}
        \setbeamercolor{block title}{bg=StanfordCardinal, fg=white}
        \setbeamercolor{block body}{bg=StanfordLight, fg=black}
        \setbeamercolor{block title alerted}{bg=StanfordWarm, fg=white}
        \setbeamercolor{block body alerted}{bg=StanfordLight, fg=black}
        \setbeamercolor{block title example}{bg=StanfordCool, fg=white}
        \setbeamercolor{block body example}{bg=StanfordLight, fg=black}
        \setbeamercolor{item}{fg=StanfordCardinal}
        \setbeamercolor{subitem}{fg=StanfordDark}
        \setbeamercolor{subsubitem}{fg=StanfordStone}

        % ── Fonts ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
        \usefonttheme{serif}
        \setbeamerfont{title}{size=\Large, series=\bfseries}
        \setbeamerfont{subtitle}{size=\normalsize}
        \setbeamerfont{frametitle}{size=\large, series=\bfseries}
        \setbeamerfont{block title}{size=\normalsize, series=\bfseries}

        % ── Section slide style ──────────────────────────────────────────────────
        \AtBeginSection[]{
          \begin{frame}
            \vfill
            \centering
            \begin{beamercolorbox}[sep=16pt,center,rounded=true,shadow=true]{title}
              \usebeamerfont{title}\insertsectionhead\par
            \end{beamercolorbox}
            \vfill
          \end{frame}
        }
        \setbeamertemplate{footline}{
          \leavevmode
          \hbox{
            \begin{beamercolorbox}[wd=.333333\paperwidth,ht=2.25ex,dp=1ex,center]{author in head/foot}
              \usebeamerfont{author in head/foot}\insertshortauthor
            \end{beamercolorbox}
            \begin{beamercolorbox}[wd=.333333\paperwidth,ht=2.25ex,dp=1ex,center]{title in head/foot}
              \usebeamerfont{title in head/foot}\insertsectionhead
            \end{beamercolorbox}
            \begin{beamercolorbox}[wd=.333333\paperwidth,ht=2.25ex,dp=1ex,center]{date in head/foot}
              \usebeamerfont{date in head/foot}\insertframenumber{} / \inserttotalframenumber\hspace*{2ex}
            \end{beamercolorbox}
          }
          \vskip0pt
              }
        
---

# Background

## Motivation

\begin{columns}[T]
\begin{column}{0.55\textwidth}

**The school meal policy landscape**

- Over **30 million** children eat school lunch daily (USDA FNS)
- Healthy, Hunger-Free Kids Act (2010) set stricter nutrition standards
- 2014: Full implementation; 2018: USDA rollbacks on whole grains, sodium
- Many districts outsource food service to **Food Service Management Companies (FSMCs)**

\end{column}
\begin{column}{0.42\textwidth}

\begin{block}{Research Questions}
\begin{enumerate}
  \item How does scoring formula design affect competition and pricing?
  \item How do these designs impact nutritional quality? 
  \item What are the downstream health effects?
\end{enumerate}
\end{block}

\end{column}
\end{columns}

## Institutional Context: The FSMC Market

**What are FSMCs?**

- Private contractors who manage school cafeterias on behalf of School Food Authorities (SFAs)
- Responsible for procurement, staffing, menu planning, and compliance
- A handful of large national firms (Aramark, Sodexo, Chartwells) dominate

\vspace{0.5em}

**Procurement process**

- SFAs issue Requests for Proposal (RFPs) with a **scoring formula** weighting cost vs. quality
- State agencies oversee compliance with federal nutrition standards via Administrative Reviews, and also enforce their own rules (more on this later)
- Contracts renewed on multi-year cycles, creating periodic competitive bidding episodes

## Data Sources

| Source | Content | Coverage |
|---|---|---|
| Michigan MDE contract lists | SFA–FSMC pairings | SY 2019–2026 |
| State FOIA requests | Bid tabs, scores, AR findings | SY 2013– |
| NCES Common Core of Data | District enrollment, demographics | Annual |
| USDA FNS | SBP/NSLP participation | Annual |
| CEP rollout records | Community Eligibility Provision adoption | 2014– |

\begin{alertblock}{Primary States}
Michigan and New Jersey — greatest volume of FSMC procurement auctions over time
\end{alertblock}

# Scoring Weirdness and Competitive Impacts
## Slide on Cost reimbursable vs other auctions

- Write still

## Typical Scoring Categories 

**A typical RFP scores bids on multiple dimensions, including:**
\begin{itemize}
  \item Total cost (almost always has the highest weight) 
  \item Guaranteed Return (FILL IN, NEED TO UNDERSTAND )
  \item Food Quality/Specifications 
  \item Menu variety 
  \item Corporate capability and experience
\end{itemize}

## How Scoring Formulas Work

**For simplicity here we focus just on quality and cost:**

$$\text{Total Score} = w_{\text{cost}} \cdot S_{\text{cost}} + w_{\text{quality}} \cdot S_{\text{quality}}$$

\vspace{0.3em}

\begin{columns}[T]
\begin{column}{0.48\textwidth}

\textcolor{StanfordCardinal}{\textbf{Cost component}}
\begin{itemize}
  \item The lowest cost bidder usually gets the maximum allowable points (e.g 5 (NJ))
  \item How other bidders are scored can either be ordinal (2nd lowest cost bidder gets 4 points) (NJ) or cardinal (2nd lowest cost bidder gets $(1 -\frac{\text{Cost} - \text{Lowest Cost}}{\text{Lowest Cost}})*5$ points) (MI)
\end{itemize}

\end{column}
\begin{column}{0.48\textwidth}

\textcolor{StanfordCool}{\textbf{Quality component}}
\begin{itemize}
  \item Subjective: menu plans, staffing ratios, experience
  \item Evaluated by a panel — open to discretion
\end{itemize}

\end{column}
\end{columns}

## Scoring Weirdness: Key Anomalies

**Empirical patterns from bid tabulation data:**

\vspace{0.4em}

1. **Bunching near thresholds** — bids cluster just below price breakpoints in the scoring formula, consistent with strategic gaming

2. **Low entry rates** — many auctions attract only 1–2 bidders, raising questions about effective competition

3. **Quality score dispersion** — identical firms receive widely different quality scores across districts in the same year

4. **Incumbent advantage** — incumbents win at significantly higher rates than predicted by scores alone

\begin{block}{Identification Strategy}
Exploit variation in cost-vs.-quality weight \emph{across districts and over time} as quasi-experimental variation in competitive conditions
\end{block}

## Competitive Impacts: Preliminary Framework

\begin{columns}[T]
\begin{column}{0.48\textwidth}

\textbf{Outcomes of interest}
\begin{itemize}
  \item Meal price per student
  \item Number of qualified bidders
  \item Incumbent retention rate
  \item Contract duration and renewal terms
\end{itemize}

\end{column}
\begin{column}{0.48\textwidth}

\textbf{Key threats to identification}
\begin{itemize}
  \item Formula weights are chosen by districts — potentially endogenous
  \item Larger or richer districts may select differently
  \item Market-level shocks (e.g., COVID supply disruptions)
\end{itemize}

\end{column}
\end{columns}

\vspace{0.5em}

\begin{exampleblock}{Proposed Design}
Difference-in-differences using staggered formula weight changes, with district fixed effects and time trends
\end{exampleblock}

# Impact of Nutritional Quality

## Measuring Nutritional Compliance

**Administrative Review (AR) process**

- Michigan MDE conducts on-site ARs of each SFA on a multi-year cycle
- Findings document violations: caloric limits, sodium thresholds, whole grain requirements, fruit/vegetable offerings
- FOIA to Michigan MDE targets AR findings since SY 2013–14

\vspace{0.4em}

**Nutrition quality measures**

| Measure | Source | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| AR violation rates | State FOIA | Direct compliance measure |
| Healthy Eating Index (HEI) | NHANES dietary recall | Population-level |
| Menu nutrient content | FSMC contract records | Where available |

## FSMC vs. Self-Operated: Nutritional Tradeoffs

\begin{columns}[T]
\begin{column}{0.55\textwidth}

\textbf{Theoretical channels}

\begin{itemize}
  \item FSMCs have \textbf{scale economies} in procurement — may access higher-quality ingredients at lower cost
  \item Profit motive may push toward \textbf{cost-cutting} on quality where compliance is difficult to monitor
  \item National firms have \textbf{standardized menus} — less local adaptation, but consistent compliance infrastructure
\end{itemize}

\end{column}
\begin{column}{0.42\textwidth}

\begin{alertblock}{Adrienne's Question}
Do school lunches meaningfully affect \textbf{short-run obesity outcomes}?

\vspace{0.3em}
\small Framing: limitation on statistical power, or a design feature requiring longer follow-up windows?
\end{alertblock}

\end{column}
\end{columns}

## HHFKA Policy Arc and Nutritional Standards

**Key regulatory milestones affecting nutritional compliance:**

\vspace{0.3em}

- **2010:** Healthy, Hunger-Free Kids Act signed — mandates new nutrition standards
- **2012:** Interim final rule published; schools begin transition
- **2014:** Full implementation — stricter caloric limits, whole grain-rich requirements, sodium targets
- **2018:** USDA rollbacks — relaxed whole grain and sodium timelines, reintroduced flavored milk

\vspace{0.3em}

\begin{block}{Research Finding from Literature}
HHFKA improved Healthy Eating Index scores among low-income students; 2018 rollbacks raised concerns about erosion of those gains (Schwartz et al.; Cohen et al.)
\end{block}

# Health Outcomes

## Linking Procurement to Health: Causal Chain

\vspace{0.2em}

$$\underbrace{\text{Scoring Formula}}_{\text{Competition}} \;\longrightarrow\; \underbrace{\text{FSMC Selection \& Pricing}}_{\text{Market Structure}} \;\longrightarrow\; \underbrace{\text{Nutritional Quality}}_{\text{Compliance}} \;\longrightarrow\; \underbrace{\text{Child Health}}_{\text{Outcomes}}$$

\vspace{0.5em}

**Each arrow represents a distinct estimand** — this project aims to identify at least the first two links, with the third as a longer-run goal

\vspace{0.3em}

\begin{columns}[T]
\begin{column}{0.48\textwidth}
\textbf{Short-run outcomes}
\begin{itemize}
  \item BMI / obesity prevalence
  \item Attendance rates
  \item Participation in NSLP/SBP
\end{itemize}
\end{column}
\begin{column}{0.48\textwidth}
\textbf{Longer-run outcomes}
\begin{itemize}
  \item Chronic disease markers
  \item Academic achievement
  \item Household food substitution (cereal demand project)
\end{itemize}
\end{column}
\end{columns}

## Community Eligibility Provision as Natural Experiment

**CEP allows high-poverty schools to offer universal free meals**

- Staggered rollout beginning 2014–15 creates plausible quasi-experimental variation
- Eliminates stigma and administrative barriers → increases participation
- Variation in timing uncorrelated with district-level health trends (conditional on controls)

\vspace{0.4em}

\begin{block}{Proposed DiD / Event Study Design}
\begin{itemize}
  \item Treatment: CEP adoption (staggered by school/district)
  \item Outcome: child health measures, meal participation, household food expenditure
  \item Controls: district FEs, year FEs, demographic trends
  \item Connected to: cereal demand project (substitution vs. complementarity)
\end{itemize}
\end{block}

## Health Outcomes: Measurement Challenges

\begin{columns}[T]
\begin{column}{0.55\textwidth}

\textbf{Data challenges}

\begin{itemize}
  \item BMI data often collected only every few years at school level
  \item NHANES is nationally representative but not district-identifiable
  \item Short panel windows limit detection of nutritional effects on obesity
  \item Selection into FSMC contracts may correlate with baseline health
\end{itemize}

\end{column}
\begin{column}{0.42\textwidth}

\begin{exampleblock}{Adrienne's Concern}
Short-run obesity effects may be \textbf{statistically underpowered} — especially if nutritional changes operate through cumulative exposure

\vspace{0.3em}
\small \textbf{Response:} Use attendance, participation, and dietary recall as \emph{intermediate} outcomes; frame obesity as a longer-horizon goal
\end{exampleblock}

\end{column}
\end{columns}

## Next Steps

\begin{columns}[T]
\begin{column}{0.48\textwidth}

\textcolor{StanfordCardinal}{\textbf{Data collection}}
\begin{itemize}
  \item FOIA to Michigan MDE: pre-2019 contract lists, AR findings (SY 2013–)
  \item Outreach to NJ counterparts
  \item Nielsen/Kilts or Circana panel data for cereal demand
\end{itemize}

\end{column}
\begin{column}{0.48\textwidth}

\textcolor{StanfordCool}{\textbf{Analysis}}
\begin{itemize}
  \item Construct panel of SFA–FSMC contracts with bid-level data
  \item Estimate scoring weight effects on entry, price, incumbent retention
  \item Link to AR compliance and CEP-based health outcomes
\end{itemize}

\end{column}
\end{columns}

\vspace{0.5em}

\begin{block}{Open Questions}
\begin{enumerate}
  \item How to operationalize Adrienne's concern about short-run obesity outcomes — limitation or design feature?
  \item What is the right counterfactual for FSMC nutritional quality (vs. self-op)?
\end{enumerate}
\end{block}

---

\begin{center}
{\Large \textcolor{StanfordCardinal}{\textbf{Thank You}}}

\vspace{1em}

{\normalsize Questions \& Discussion}

\vspace{1.5em}

\textcolor{StanfordStone}{\small aswen@stanford.edu}
\end{center}
